summary:
[L13 - Methodologies.pdf] METHODOLOGIES BAXI 3113 – INTELLIGENT AGENT Dr. Nur Zareen Zulkarnain Department of Intelligent Computing and Analytics (ICA) DG/1 -18 zareen@utem.edu.my 1 LEARNING OUTCOMES At the end of this lecture, you should be able to: •Understand the need of agent -oriented methodologies •State different types of agent -oriented methodologies •Explain mobile agents by discussing the needs, issues and typology associated to it •Differentiate Telescript and TCL/TK 2 AGENT ORIENTED METHODOLOGIES •There is need for agent -oriented methodologies → centred around specific agent -oriented abstractions •The adoption of OO methodologies would produce mismatches - Classes, objects, client -servers: little to do with agents 3 AGENT ORIENTED METHODOLOGIES •Each methodology may introduce further abstractions - around which to model software and to organize the software process •For example: roles, organizations, responsibilities, belief, desire and intentions •Not directly translating into concrete entities of the software system → the concept of role is an aspect of an agent , not an agent 4 AGENT -BASED ANALYSIS •Analysis aims to understand , at least •What are the main actors interacting with the system •How the system interacts with these actors •What the system is supposed to do •Then, we associate accordingly •Roles responsibilities and capabilities •Interaction patterns between agents 5 AGENT -BASED DESIGN •Design aims to engineer , at least •What are the main components interacting within the system •What are the responsibilities and the capabilities of each component in the system •How the components interact to implement the system, i.e., the architecture of the system •Differently from analysis: we need to choose on which agents to use and how they interact 6 RELEVANT METHODOLOGIES •GAIA Methodologies •Encourages a developer to think of building agent -based systems as a process of organisational design •Agent UML •Extend Unified Modeling Language (UML) with agent features •Prometeus •Focuses mainly on the internal agent architecture → basically a methodology for designing BDI agent systems 7 MOBILE AGENTS 8 9REMOTE PROCEDURE CALLSclient computer server computer network client process server process MOBILE AGENTSclient computer server computer networkclient process serverprocessagent vs MOBILE AGENTS Why mobile agents? •low-bandwidth networks •efficient use of network resources There are many issues when building software tools that can support mobile agents: •security for hosts and agents •heterogeneity of hosts •dynamic linking 10 MOBILE AGENT TYPOLOGY 11 We can divide mobile agents into at least three types Autonomous On-demand ‘Active mail’ AUTONOMOUS •By autonomous mobile, we mean agents that are able to decide for themselves where to go, when, and what to do when they get there (subject to certain resource constraints) •Such agents are generally programmed in a special language that provides a go instruction •best known example is TELESCRIPT 12 ON-DEMAND •The idea is that a host is only required to execute an agent when it explicitly demands the agent •Best known example → JAVA in html •A user with a JAVA -compatible browser can request html pages that contain applets – small programs implemented in the JAVA language •These applets are downloaded along with all other images, text, forms, etc., on the page, and, once downloaded, are executed on the user’s machine 13 ‘ACTIVE MAIL’ •The idea here is to ‘piggy -back’ agent programs onto mail •The best -known example of this work is the mime extension to email, allowing Safe -TCL scripts to be sent •When email is received, the ‘agent’ is unpacked, and the script executed → hence the email is no longer passive, but active 14 TELESCRIPT AND TCL/TK 15 TELESCRIPT •TELESCRIPT is a language -based environment for constructing mobile agent systems •There are two key concepts in TELESCRIPT technology: •Places → virtual locations occupied by agents.

[L1- Introduction to Agents (1).pdf] INTRODUCTION TO AGENTS BAXI 3113 – INTELLIGENT AGENT Dr. Nur Zareen Zulkarnain Department of Intelligent Computing and Analytics (ICA) DG/1 -18 zareen@utem.edu.my 1 Students will be exposed to the concept of intelligent agent and multiagent systems including: theory of agents common agent architectures methods of cooperationmethods of communication potential applications for agentsCOURSE OVERVIEW 2 COURSE LEARNING OUTCOMES Upon completing this subject, you should be able to: CLO1: Evaluate various concepts of intelligent agent. CLO2: Organise effective solution steps in solving intelligent agent problems.

[L2.0 - Intelligent Agents (1).pdf] INTELLIGENT AGENTS BAXI 3113 – INTELLIGENT AGENT Dr. Nur Zareen Zulkarnain Department of Intelligent Computing and Analytics (ICA) DG/1 -18 zareen@utem.edu.my 1 LEARNING OUTCOMES At the end of today’s lecture, you should be able to: •Recognise agent behaviours •Compare agent with objects and AI systems •Justify agents as intentional system •Identify tasks for agents 2 WHAT IS AN AGENT? 3 WHAT IS AN AGENT?
